title: What Are the Differences Between WAEC, NECO, and JAMB?
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You are a secondary school student in Nigeria, and you are hearing about WAEC, NECO, and JAMB. Your teachers mention them. Your parents ask about them. Your friends are registering for them. But no one has clearly explained what each exam is for, how they differ, and why you need to take all of them. The confusion is understandable. These exams serve different purposes, are administered by different bodies, and are required at different stages of your academic journey. Here is a clear breakdown of what each exam actually is and how they fit together.

WAEC: The West African Senior School Certificate Examination

WAEC stands for the West African Examinations Council. It administers the West African Senior School Certificate Examination, or WASSCE, to students in Nigeria, Ghana, Sierra Leone, Liberia, and The Gambia. The exam is taken at the end of secondary school, typically in the third term of SS3. It is the most widely recognized secondary school leaving certificate in West Africa.

There are two versions of the WAEC exam. The school candidate exam, often called the May or June WAEC, is taken by students who are enrolled in secondary schools and have completed the SS1 to SS3 curriculum. The private candidate exam, often called the November or December WAEC or GCE, is taken by students who have already left secondary school and want to improve their results or sit for subjects they missed. Both versions produce the same certificate, but the registration process and timing differ.

WAEC is required for admission to universities, polytechnics, and colleges of education across Nigeria and West Africa. Most institutions require at least five credit passes, including English Language and Mathematics, in no more than two sittings. This means you can combine results from two different WAEC exams, or from a WAEC and a NECO exam, to meet the requirement. The key phrase is credit pass. A credit pass is a grade of C6 or higher. Anything below C6 is not considered a credit and will not count toward your five required credits for admission.

NECO: The National Examinations Council

NECO stands for the National Examinations Council. It administers the Senior School Certificate Examination, or SSCE, to students in Nigeria only. Like WAEC, it is taken at the end of secondary school. The content and syllabus are similar to WAEC, and the certificate is equally recognized by Nigerian universities and employers.

The main difference between WAEC and NECO is the examining body. WAEC is a regional body serving multiple West African countries. NECO is a national body serving Nigeria alone. Some students find NECO slightly less difficult than WAEC, though this varies by subject and by year. The grading systems are similar but not identical. Both use a scale from A1 to F9, but the conversion of raw scores to grades may differ slightly between the two bodies.

Why take both? Because universities allow you to combine results. If you perform poorly in a subject on WAEC, you can retake that subject through NECO and use the better grade. Having two sets of results gives you flexibility. Many students register for both exams in the same year, taking WAEC in May or June and NECO in July. The schedule is tight, but the strategy is sound. If one exam does not go well, the other provides a backup.

JAMB: The Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board

JAMB is fundamentally different from WAEC and NECO. It is not a secondary school leaving examination. It is a university entrance examination. JAMB administers the Unified Tertiary Matriculation Examination, or UTME, which is required for admission to Nigerian universities, polytechnics, and colleges of education. You cannot gain admission to a Nigerian tertiary institution without a JAMB score.

The UTME tests four subjects relevant to your intended course of study. All candidates take English Language, which is compulsory. The remaining three subjects depend on your chosen course. A student applying for medicine might take Biology, Chemistry, and Physics. A student applying for law might take Literature in English, Government, and Christian or Islamic Religious Studies. A student applying for accounting might take Mathematics, Economics, and Commerce. Your subject combination must match the requirements of your intended course and institution.

The JAMB score is a number between zero and four hundred. Each subject is scored out of one hundred, and the four scores are added together. Most universities set a cut-off mark, typically between 180 and 200, though competitive courses at top universities require scores of 250 and above. Your JAMB score, combined with your WAEC or NECO results and your performance in the university's own post-UTME screening, determines whether you receive admission.

How the Three Exams Fit Together

Think of WAEC and NECO as the foundation. They certify that you have completed secondary education and meet the basic academic requirements for university admission. Think of JAMB as the gateway. It determines which university and which course you qualify for. You need both. A strong WAEC or NECO result without a good JAMB score will not get you into university. A good JAMB score without the required WAEC or NECO credits will not get you in either.

The typical timeline looks like this. In SS3, you register for WAEC in the first term. The exam is taken in May or June. NECO is taken in July. JAMB registration opens around January and the exam is typically taken between March and May, before WAEC in some years and after in others. The timing varies, so check the official announcements each year. After results are released, universities begin their admission processes, which include post-UTME screenings, usually between July and October.

The Bottom Line

WAEC and NECO are secondary school leaving examinations that certify your completion of secondary education. JAMB is a university entrance examination that determines your eligibility for admission. Most students take both WAEC and NECO to give themselves the flexibility to combine results. All three exams are important, but they serve different purposes. Understand what each one is for, and plan your preparation accordingly.
